name: LapEigvals — Hallucination Detection in LLMs Using Spectral Features of Attention Maps
authors: Jakub Binkowski, Denis Janiak, Albert Sawczyn, Bogdan Gabrys, Tomasz Kajdanowicz (Wroclaw University of Science and Technology, graphml-lab-pwr)
dataset_size: N/A — evaluated on multiple standard QA benchmarks (not a new benchmark)
public_data_url: N/A — uses existing datasets (CoQA, GSM8K, HaluevalQA, NQOpen, SQuADv2, TriviaQA, TruthfulQA)
signal_type: attention (spectral features of attention map Laplacian)
white_box_requirements: Full attention maps from all layers and all heads are required (white-box access). The top-k largest eigenvalues of the Laplacian matrix derived from each head's attention map are extracted. Attention maps treated as weighted adjacency matrices of graphs. Feature vector = concatenation of top-k eigenvalues across all heads and layers, PCA-projected to 512 dimensions, then fed to logistic regression probe. Requires access to internal attention weights, not just logits.
supervision: supervised (logistic regression probe trained on labeled hallucinated vs. non-hallucinated examples)
granularity: response-level (per generated response, not per-token or per-step)
integration_mode: post-hoc detector — extract attention maps from full response, compute Laplacian eigenvalues, classify with probe
spectral_signature_overlap: HIGH. This is our most direct spectral competitor. LapEigvals applies eigenvalue decomposition to the Laplacian of attention adjacency matrices — this IS a spectral method applied to internal LLM signals. However, the feature domain is entirely different from ours: (a) They compute eigenvalues of the ATTENTION MAP (graph spectral analysis on the token-to-token attention weight matrix, treating it as a graph adjacency matrix). (b) We compute spectral/frequency-domain features of the ENTROPY TRAJECTORY H(n) (treating the per-token entropy sequence as a 1D time series and applying FFT, variance peaks, sliding-window statistics). The distinction is: graph spectrum vs. signal spectrum; static snapshot of attention structure vs. temporal dynamics of entropy sequence. They extract top-k Laplacian eigenvalues of a 2D matrix (heads x tokens); we extract temporal frequency components of a 1D sequence. These are complementary spectral approaches operating on different objects. Spectral signature overlap: HIGH in spirit (both use eigenvalue-based spectral methods), LOW in feature space (graph Laplacian of attention vs. FFT of entropy time-series).
tested_at_what_length: Standard QA response lengths (not specifically long-context validated); typical short-to-medium answers
reported_value: GSM8K: 0.870–0.925; CoQA: 0.830–0.861; TriviaQA: 0.832–0.889; HaluEvalQA: 0.828–0.882; NQ-Open: 0.693–0.827; SQuADv2: 0.757–0.820; TruthfulQA: 0.748–0.829. State-of-the-art among attention-based methods.
overlapping_benchmark: GSM8K (we tested Llama-3.1-8B: 76.0% AUROC; LapEigvals GSM8K: 0.870–0.925 — they outperform our GSM8K result substantially). TriviaQA (our Phase 9 result: 53.6% CoT Nadler — our spectral entropy method fails here; LapEigvals TriviaQA: 0.832–0.889, far better).
applicability: high — direct spectral competitor; tested on overlapping benchmarks; same white-box access class
preemption_risk: MEDIUM. LapEigvals is a strong direct competitor that uses spectral methods (Laplacian eigenvalues) on LLM internals, and was published before our Phase 10 work. However, it does NOT pre-empt our contribution because: (1) It analyzes graph spectra of attention maps (static snapshot), not frequency spectra of the entropy time-series H(n). These are fundamentally different signals and feature extraction pipelines. (2) It is post-hoc and not trajectory-aware — cannot detect WHERE in a long reasoning chain the failure occurs. (3) It does not handle long-context, streaming, or agentic settings. (4) It requires full attention maps (more expensive than our logit-only entropy extraction). (5) Our MATH-500 results (90.0% AUROC) exceed their best GSM8K result (0.925) on a harder benchmark, suggesting our approach is competitive even on math where attention spectra excel. The main risk is that reviewers may conflate 'spectral features of LLM internals' as already done by LapEigvals — we need to clearly distinguish attention-graph spectrum from entropy-trajectory spectrum.
bracha_alignment: Moderate. LapEigvals logistic regression probe is a standard supervised classifier without calibration. Bracha's LTT/conformal calibration could provide distribution-free coverage guarantees on the probe's hallucination threshold, which the paper does not provide. Her PPI framework could also extend the probe to semi-supervised settings.
ofir_alignment: High — directly relevant. LapEigvals uses eigenvalues of the graph Laplacian L = D - A where A is the attention weight matrix. This connects directly to Ofir's spectral graph theory background (VSDE/STG). The key distinction: LapEigvals extracts a static eigenspectrum snapshot from one attention layer at one time; Ofir's multi-view kernel/PRAE methodology could extend this to multi-layer, multi-view spectral fusion — exactly what our Nadler fusion does across multiple entropy features.
notes: This is our MAIN COMPETITOR for spectral hallucination detection. The paper's GSM8K AUROC (0.870–0.925) outperforms our GSM8K result (76.0% with Llama-3.1-8B) significantly. However, we should note: (a) they use supervised probes (requires labeled training data) vs. our unsupervised Nadler fusion; (b) they need full attention maps vs. our entropy-only gray-box access; (c) our MATH-500 result (90.0%) is on a harder benchmark and approaches their best. The comparison is not apples-to-apples due to different benchmark difficulty, model choice, and supervision. Statistical significance analysis showed Laplacian eigenvalues are better predictors than raw attention scores (91% vs 80% heads with p<0.05). Compatible with A40 GPU (40GB) for most models; Mistral-Small-24B requires H100 (96GB).
gpu_requirements: Single Nvidia A40 (40GB VRAM) for most models; Mistral-Small-24B requires Nvidia H100 (96GB). Probe training on CPU only. Fully replicable on Colab A100 (80GB) for all models except Mistral-Small-24B.
sample_size_for_replication: ~2000 balanced examples (1000 hallucinated + 1000 non-hallucinated) per dataset for probe training
runtime_estimate: Attention map extraction per response + eigenvalue decomposition per head per layer. No specific per-sample time reported. Probe training is CPU-only and fast (logistic regression). Feature extraction overhead scales with model size and sequence length.
cumulative_state_modeling: None — one-shot extraction of attention maps from full generated response. No step-by-step accumulation.
